subtopics: AR $\cdot$ MA $\cdot$ ARMA $\cdot$ Stationarity $\cdot$ Unit Roots $\cdot$ Optimal Forecasting
prerequisites: Topic 17ÔÇô18 ÔÇö Linear Regression; Topic 5 ÔÇö Expectation & Variance
learningObjectives: **Derive the stationarity condition, variance, and ACF** for an AR(1) process, and explain what happens when $|\phi|\geq1$.
**Contrast a random walk with a stationary AR(1)**: compute the mean and variance of each as functions of time, and sketch their ACFs.
**Compute the ACF of an MA(1)** from first principles and verify the cutoff property at lag 1.

**Identify the correct ARMA($p$,$q$) model** from the pattern of ACF and PACF: AR($p$) $\leftrightarrow$ PACF cuts at $p$; MA($q$) $\leftrightarrow$ ACF cuts at $q$; ARMA $\leftrightarrow$ both decay.
**Explain the unit root concept and the ADF test**: state the null hypothesis, explain why the standard $t$-distribution does not apply, and discuss the practical importance for financial series.
**Compute optimal multi-step forecasts and their error variance** for an AR(1) process.

blocks.title: Covariance Stationarity and ARMA
blocks.content:
A process $(X_{t})$ is **covariance-stationary** (weakly stationary) if: $\mathbb{E}[X_{t}] = \mu$ (constant), $\text{Var}(X_{t}) = \sigma^{2} < \infty$ (constant), and $\text{Cov}(X_{t}, X_{t-k})$ depends only on $|k|$, not on $t$.

**AR($p$):** $X_{t} = \phi_{1} X_{t-1}+\cdots+\phi_{p} X_{t-p}+\varepsilon_{t}$. Stationary iff all roots of $1-\phi_{1} z-\cdots-\phi_{p} z^{p}=0$ lie outside the unit circle.

**MA($q$):** $X_{t} = \varepsilon_{t}+\theta_{1}\varepsilon_{t-1}+\cdots +\theta_{q}\varepsilon_{t-q}$. Always stationary.

**ARMA($p$,$q$):** combines both. Stationarity governed by the AR part.

**ACF** $\rho(k)=\text{Cov}(X_{t},X_{t-k})/\text{Var}(X_{t})$. **PACF** $\alpha(k)$ = partial autocorrelation at lag $k$ (effect of $X_{t-k}$ on $X_{t}$ after removing intermediate lags).

blocks.solution:
**(a) Stationarity.** The AR(1) characteristic equation is $1-\phi z=0$, root $z=1/\phi$. Stationarity requires this root to lie outside the unit circle: $|1/\phi|>1$, i.e.\ $|\phi|<1$.

\[\boxed{|\phi|<1}\]

**(b) Moments under stationarity.**

**Mean:** Taking expectations of $X_{t}=\phi X_{t-1}+\varepsilon_{t}$: $\mathbb{E}[X_{t}]=\phi\mathbb{E}[X_{t-1}]+0$. By stationarity $\mathbb{E}[X_{t}]=\mathbb{E}[X_{t-1}]=\mu$, so $\mu=\phi\mu \Rightarrow \mu(1-\phi)=0 \Rightarrow \mu=0$.

**Variance:** $\text{Var}(X_{t})=\phi^{2}\text{Var}(X_{t-1})+\sigma^{2}=\phi^{2}\gamma_{0}+\sigma^{2}$. By stationarity $\gamma_{0}=\phi^{2}\gamma_{0}+\sigma^{2}$: \[\text{Var}(X_{t}) = \gamma_{0} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{1-\phi^{2}}\]

**Autocovariance:** $\gamma_{k} = \text{Cov}(X_{t},X_{t-k})$. For $k\geq1$: $\gamma_{k} = \text{Cov}(\phi X_{t-1}+\varepsilon_{t}, X_{t-k}) = \phi\text{Cov}(X_{t-1},X_{t-k})+0 = \phi\gamma_{k-1}$. Solving: $\gamma_{k} = \phi^{k}\gamma_{0}$.

**ACF:** \[\rho(k) = \frac{\gamma_{k}}{\gamma_{0}}= \phi^{k}, \quad k=0,1,2,ÔÇŽ\] The ACF decays *geometrically* at rate $|\phi|$.

**(c) $|\phi|>1$ ÔÇö explosive process.** The process is non-stationary: the variance grows without bound as $t\to\infty$. Starting from any $X_{0}$, $\text{Var}(X_{t})$ grows like $\phi^{2t}\text{Var}(X_{0})+\sigma^{2}(1-\phi^{2t})/(1-\phi^{2})$. The process ÔÇťblows upÔÇŁ exponentially. Such processes are not useful models for financial time series.

**Key takeaway:** AR(1) is stationary iff $|\phi|<1$; then $\text{Var}=\sigma^{2}/(1-\phi^{2})$ and ACF $=\phi^{k}$ (geometric decay). $|\phi|=1$ gives a random walk (unit root, non-stationary).

blocks.content:
**ACF of MA(1): Computation and Cutoff.** $X_{t} = \varepsilon_{t} + \theta\varepsilon_{t-1}$, $\varepsilon_{t}\stackrel{\text{iid}}{\sim}(0,\sigma^{2})$. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph{*})]\item Compute $\gamma(k) = \text{Cov}(X_{t}, X_{t-k})$ for all $k$.

\item Compute the ACF $\rho(k)$.

\item Verify the cutoff property: ACF $= 0$ for $k\geq2$.

\item Compare to the AR(1) ACF.\end{enumerate}
blocks.solution:
**(a) Autocovariance.** $X_{t} = \varepsilon_{t}+\theta\varepsilon_{t-1}$. Note $\mathbb{E}[X_{t}]=0$ and $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_{s},\varepsilon_{r})=\sigma^{2}\mathbf{1}[s=r]$. **$k=0$:** \[\gamma(0) = \text{Var}(X_{t}) = \text{Var}(\varepsilon_{t})+\theta^{2}\text{Var}(\varepsilon_{t-1}) = \sigma^{2}(1+\theta^{2})\] **$k=1$:** \[\gamma(1) = \text{Cov}(\varepsilon_{t}+\theta\varepsilon_{t-1},\; \varepsilon_{t-1}+\theta\varepsilon_{t-2}) = \theta\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_{t-1},\varepsilon_{t-1}) = \theta\sigma^{2}\] **$k\geq2$:** $X_{t}$ depends only on $\varepsilon_{t},\varepsilon_{t-1}$ and $X_{t-k}$ depends on $\varepsilon_{t-k},\varepsilon_{t-k-1}$. For $k\geq2$, all four indices are distinct, so: \[\gamma(k) = 0, \quad k\geq2\] **(b) ACF.** \[\rho(0) = 1, \qquad \rho(1) = \frac{\theta\sigma^{2}}{\sigma^{2}(1+\theta^{2})}= \frac{\theta}{1+\theta^{2}}, \qquad \rho(k) = 0\ \text{for }k\geq2\] **(c) Cutoff at lag 1.** For $|k|\geq2$, $X_{t}$ and $X_{t-k}$ share no common $\varepsilon$ terms, so they are *uncorrelated* (not just asymptotically but exactly). The ACF has a *hard cutoff* at lag 1. Ôťô **Note:** $|\rho(1)|\leq1/2$, with equality when $\theta=\pm1$. This is because $\theta/(1+\theta^{2})\leq1/2$ for all real $\theta$ (AM-GM: $1+\theta^{2}\geq2|\theta|$). **(d) Comparison to AR(1).**
MA(1): $X_{t}=\varepsilon_{t}+\theta\varepsilon_{t-1}$AR(1): $X_{t}=\phi X_{t-1}+\varepsilon_{t}$
$\rho(1)$$\theta/(1+\theta^{2})$$\phi$
$\rho(k\geq2)$$0$ (exact cutoff)$\phi^{k}$ (geometric decay)
MemoryFinite (lag 1 only)Infinite (all lags)
IdentificationACF cuts offACF decays, PACF cuts off
**Key takeaway:** MA($q$) has ACF that cuts off exactly at lag $q$; AR($p$) has ACF that decays geometrically. This is the primary ACF diagnostic for model identification.

sections.title: Model Identification, Unit Roots, and Forecasting

blocks.content:
**ARMA Model Identification from ACF/PACF.** \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph{*})]\item Scenario A: ACF decays exponentially, PACF cuts off after lag 2. What model?

\item Scenario B: ACF cuts off after lag 1, PACF decays. What model?

\item Scenario C: Both ACF and PACF decay. What model?

\item Summarise the general ARMA($p$,$q$) identification rules.\end{enumerate}
blocks.solution:
**(a) Scenario A:** ACF decays exponentially + PACF cuts off at lag 2. \[\Rightarrow **AR(2)**\] AR($p$) processes have: PACF cuts off at lag $p$ (exactly zero after); ACF decays geometrically (exponentially or oscillating). **(b) Scenario B:** ACF cuts off at lag 1 + PACF decays. \[\Rightarrow **MA(1)**\] MA($q$) processes have: ACF cuts off at lag $q$; PACF decays (tailing off, possibly oscillating). **(c) Scenario C:** Both ACF and PACF decay. \[\Rightarrow **ARMA($p$,$q$) for some $p,q\geq1$**\] When both decay, the process has both AR and MA components. Use information criteria (AIC, BIC) to select $p$ and $q$. **(d) Identification rules.**
ACFPACFModel
Cuts off at lag $q$DecaysMA($q$)
DecaysCuts off at lag $p$AR($p$)
Both decayBoth decayARMA($p$,$q$)
Cuts off at 0Cuts off at 0White noise
**Practical note.** ÔÇťCuts offÔÇŁ means the ACF/PACF is statistically indistinguishable from zero beyond a certain lag (within $\pm1.96/\sqrt{T}$ bands). ÔÇťDecaysÔÇŁ means it tapers toward zero but does not reach it within a few lags. **Key takeaway:** The BoxÔÇôJenkins methodology: (1) check ACF/PACF patterns; (2) identify model class; (3) estimate; (4) check residuals. AR($p$) has characteristic PACF cutoff; MA($q$) has characteristic ACF cutoff.

blocks.content:
**Unit Root and DickeyÔÇôFuller Test.** \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph{*})]\item What is a unit root in AR(1)?

\item Explain the ADF test. What is $H_{0}$?

\item Why doesn't the standard $t$-distribution apply?

\item Why does stationarity testing matter for financial series?\end{enumerate}
blocks.solution:
**(a) Unit root.** The AR(1) $X_{t}=\phi X_{t-1}+\varepsilon_{t}$ has a **unit root** when $\phi=1$ (the characteristic root $1/\phi=1$ lies *on* the unit circle). The process becomes a random walk: $X_{t} = X_{t-1}+\varepsilon_{t}$, which is non-stationary.

**(b) Augmented DickeyÔÇôFuller (ADF) test.** Rewrite the AR(1) as: \[\Delta X_{t} = X_{t} - X_{t-1}= (\phi-1)X_{t-1}+\varepsilon_{t} = \delta X_{t-1}+\varepsilon_{t}\] where $\delta = \phi-1$.

$H_{0}\colon\delta=0$ (unit root, $\phi=1$, non-stationary) vs $H_{1}\colon\delta<0$ ($\phi<1$, stationary).

The ADF test augments this regression with lagged differences $\Delta X_{t-1},ÔÇŽ,\Delta X_{t-p}$ to absorb serial correlation in residuals: \[\Delta X_{t} = \delta X_{t-1}+ \sum_{j=1}^{p}\gamma_{j}\Delta X_{t-j}+ \varepsilon_{t}\] The test statistic is the $t$-ratio on $\hat{\delta}$.

**(c) Why not the standard $t$-distribution?** Under $H_{0}$ ($\phi=1$), $X_{t}$ is non-stationary. The standard OLS inference theory relies on stationarity and CLT arguments that break down when $\phi=1$. Under the unit root null, $\hat{\delta}$ converges at rate $T$ (not $\sqrt{T}$), and the test statistic converges to a functional of **Brownian motion**, known as the **DickeyÔÇôFuller distribution**. This distribution is *left-skewed* and has more negative critical values than the standard $t$ (e.g.\ the 5\% critical value is approximately $-2.86$ for a driftless model vs $-1.65$ for a one-sided $t$).

**(d) Practical importance for financial time series.** \begin{itemize}\item **Stock prices:** typically non-stationary (random walk). Running regressions on levels leads to *spurious regression* (artificially high $R^{2}$ and significant $t$-statistics with no real relationship).

\item **Returns:** typically stationary (mean-reverting after demeaning). Safe to use in standard regression and time series models.

\item **Cointegration:** two non-stationary series can have a stationary linear combination (cointegrated), which is exploitable for pairs trading.

\item **Correct differencing:** if a series is I(1) (integrated order 1), first-differencing gives a stationary series. Using levels in a regression of an I(1) series on another I(1) series (not cointegrated) produces spurious results.\end{itemize}

**Key takeaway:** Unit root $\Leftrightarrow$ $\phi=1$ $\Leftrightarrow$ non-stationary. The ADF test has $H_{0}:$ unit root (test on $\hat{\delta}$ from $\Delta X_{t} = \delta X_{t-1}+ÔÇŽ$). Non-standard distribution because OLS inference breaks under non-stationarity. In finance, test whether to use prices (likely I(1)) or returns (likely I(0)).

blocks.content:
**Optimal Forecasting with AR(1).** $X_{t} = 0.6 X_{t-1}+\varepsilon_{t}$, $\sigma^{2}=1$, $X_{100}=5$. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph{*})]\item 1-step-ahead forecast $\mathbb{E}[X_{101}\mid X_{100}=5]$.

\item $h$-step-ahead forecast $\mathbb{E}[X_{100+h}\mid X_{100}=5]$.

\item What does the forecast converge to as $h\to\infty$?

\item Forecast error variance for the $h$-step forecast.\end{enumerate}
blocks.solution:
**(a) 1-step-ahead forecast.** \[\mathbb{E}[X_{101}\mid X_{100}=5] = 0.6\times5 = \boxed{3}\] **(b) $h$-step-ahead forecast.** Unrolling the recursion: $X_{100+h}= 0.6^{h} X_{100}+ \sum_{j=0}^{h-1}0.6^{j}\varepsilon_{100+h-j}$. Since future innovations are independent of $X_{100}$: \[\mathbb{E}[X_{100+h}\mid X_{100}=5] = 0.6^{h}\times5 = \boxed{5\times0.6^h}\]
$h$Forecast $= 5\times0.6^{h}$
13.000
21.800
50.389
100.030
**(c) Long-run convergence.** \[5\times0.6^{h} \to 0 \quad\text{as }h\to\infty\] The forecast converges to the unconditional mean $\mu=0$ (since the process has mean zero). The rate of convergence is geometric with factor $0.6$ per period. **How quickly?** After $h=5$ steps, the forecast is $0.389$ (7.8\% of the initial value). After $h=10$, it is $0.030$ (0.6\%). The ÔÇťhalf-lifeÔÇŁ is $h_{1/2}=\log(0.5)/\log(0.6)\approx1.36$ steps. **(d) Forecast error variance.** The $h$-step forecast error is: $X_{100+h}-\hat{X}_{100+h}= \sum_{j=0}^{h-1}0.6^{j}\varepsilon_{100+h-j}$. \[\text{Var}(X_{100+h}-\hat{X}_{100+h}) = \sigma^{2}\sum_{j=0}^{h-1}0.6^{2j}= \sigma^{2}\cdot\frac{1-0.6^{2h}}{1-0.36}= \frac{1-0.36^{h}}{0.64}\]
$h$Forecast error variance
1$1.000$(just $\sigma^{2}$)
2$1.360$
5$1.553$
$\infty$$1.563 = 1/0.64 = \sigma^{2}/(1-\phi^{2})$(unconditional variance)
The forecast error variance starts at $\sigma^{2}=1$ for $h=1$ and converges to the unconditional variance $\sigma^{2}/(1-\phi^{2})=1/0.64 \approx1.563$ as $h\to\infty$. **Key takeaway:** AR(1) forecasts mean-revert at rate $\phi^{h}$; forecast error variance grows from $\sigma^{2}$ toward $\sigma^{2}/(1-\phi^{2})$. At short horizons, the model provides useful signal; at long horizons, the forecast converges to the unconditional mean with full unconditional uncertainty.

blocks.content:
**Topic 19 Technique Toolkit**
AR(1) stationarity$|\phi|<1$. $\text{Var}=\sigma^{2}/(1-\phi^{2})$. ACF $\rho(k)=\phi^{k}$ (geometric decay).
Random walk$\phi=1$. $\text{Var}(Y_{t})=t$ (grows). ACF $\approx 1$ for all lags. Best forecast = current value.
MA(1) ACF$\rho(1)=\theta/(1+\theta^{2})$, $\rho(k)=0$ for $k\geq2$. Hard cutoff at lag 1.
Model identificationAR($p$): PACF cuts at $p$, ACF decays. MA($q$): ACF cuts at $q$, PACF decays. ARMA: both decay.
ADF test$H_{0}$: unit root ($\delta=0$ in $\Delta X_{t}=\delta X_{t-1}+ÔÇŽ$). Use DickeyÔÇôFuller critical values, not $t$.
Finance applicationPrices: I(1) ÔÇö use returns (first differences). Spurious regression risk with non-stationary series.
AR(1) $h$-step forecast$\hat{X}_{t+h}=\phi^{h} X_{t}$. Converges to 0 (mean) as $h\to\infty$.
Forecast error variance$\sigma^{2}(1-\phi^{2h})/(1-\phi^{2})$. Grows from $\sigma^{2}$ to $\sigma^{2}/(1-\phi^{2})$.

problemIndex.name: ACF of MA(1): Computation and Cutoff
